author: @Omnesum
text:
I was just watching a video by Nighthawkinlight about flash graphene. This was my suggestion to the video, that might or might not work but seemed steampunk enough that you might appreciate it.
"How long would it take to recharge that capacitor bank once fired? Big idea suggestion is to connect a wimhurst generator to the bank, with a wind turbine turning it. Then  multiply the number of coffee tubes in a rotating...? (I'm visualizing bullets in a revolver) piece. When the bank is fully charged instead of the spark gap usually on the generator it flashes the tube, rotates to the next one, and the bank refills. Theoretically you could do all this from the safety of the other side of the room"

author: @grehuy
text: Robert, you never mention a complete different approach to graphene production which is HTC. There are astonishing results of carbonizing simple starch at only about 230C which produces very beautiful huge nano spheres of aromatic molecules which could be reduced in further processes to carbon nano spheres by ridding them of H and O complexes. Since these spheres form so easily, there should be a path of growing graphene sheets like other crystals out of a "melt" (starch for ex or Phenol or..).

author: @ThinkingandTinkering
text: the difference between the two is the same as the difference between graphite and graphene. It's to do with the number of layers. one layer is graphene multi layers are graphite. Graphene oxide is what you need. If you get graphite oxide you will need to dissolve it and sonicate it to graphene oxide before you use it.

author: @fusiondecor
text:
Thank you,
Would it be possible to make a graphene component that can be mixed in the resin, so you would create a paintable high impact coating and if possible how would I go about that?

author: @ThinkingandTinkering
text: Use a lightweight carrier lay. Impregnate it with grapheme oxide - bake it to reduce the oxide to grapheme and use this between your two sandwich layers

author: @fusiondecor
text:
Hi Robert,
Nice video's, also the High Voltage projects are great, but now I'm after graphene for strength. I'd like to make graphene epoxy armour coating for my RC foam plane models.
Would you have any suggestions about how to go about making the grapene component for this?
The process to apply the grapene to the foam model would be in between two separate epoxy coatings so the grapene would not have to be mixed in the epoxy resin.
Looking forwards seeing more of your video's

author: @ThinkingandTinkering
text: No, not really - super caps have the requirements of conductivity, large surface area, pseudocapacitance - GICs don't have a particularly good SA compared even to activated carbon.

author: @Tachyoncatcher
text:
Cheers Rob,  I find you educational AND entertaining.  I love the direction you are going with this ;)  You are a generous man.  I have wondered if the camera flash method of production would have a greater appeal to the home experimenter as anyone can get their hands on a camera flash.  I will admit, I am ignorant of the preparation processes for the camera flash method.  You are doing a bang up job with these videos.  Keep up the good work.
Randy

author: @ThinkingandTinkering
text: with acetic acid to form calcium acetate and carbon dioxide and water. The calcium carbonate is non soluable in water whereas the calcium acetate is. As the calcium and acetate ions migrate out of the cuttlefish bone structure the hit isolated pockets of carbonic acid (formed by dissolving carbon dioxide in water) of sufficient concentration to reverse the reaction and form dendrites of calcium carbonate again. If you leave it long enough or up the acetic acid concentration this effect will go.

author: @ThinkingandTinkering
text: Lol - They used to be my favorite group. Chemists are to blame for this - we often see reactions only in one direction and that's how we write it. A group of starting materials on one side an arrow and a group of products on the other side. We write it as if that's all that can happen. In truth all reactions are reversible and all reactions exist in equilibrium. Nothing goes to completion. What you are doing when you dissolve cuttlefishbone in vinegar is reacting the calcium carbonate
